nodes.title: Build a type-safe Ecto-inspired ORM in Temper
nodes.description:
Build an ORM in the Temper language that prevents SQL injection at the type level. Inspired by Elixir's Ecto, featuring: Schema (SafeIdentifier, TableDef, FieldDef), composable immutable Query builder, Changeset (cast→validate→SQL pipeline), and safe SQL generation via secure-composition's SqlBuilder/SqlFragment.

The security model relies on sealed interfaces and validated identifiers — no raw string can reach SQL generation without going through SafeIdentifier validation.

nodes.title: Implement full ORM: Schema, Query, Changeset, Orm modules with tests
nodes.description:
Initial implementation of the complete ORM in Temper's literate markdown format (.temper.md):

- schema.temper.md: SafeIdentifier (sealed interface with validated constructor), TableDef, FieldDef with typed field variants (StringField, IntField, BoolField, etc.)
- query.temper.md: Composable immutable Query class with where(), select(), orderBy(), limit(), offset(), toSql()/safeToSql() — user values only enter via SqlFragment, identifiers via SafeIdentifier
- changeset.temper.md: Sealed Changeset interface with cast→validate→SQL pipeline. Params hidden from external code. Exhaustive when dispatch on FieldType for SQL generation.
- orm.temper.md: deleteSql helper using SqlBuilder
- Tests: changeset_test, query_test, schema_test

Sources:
- 328d02d: 'initial' — all 1017 lines of ORM code and tests

nodes.title: ORM code complete but depends on external secure-composition
nodes.description:
The ORM implementation is complete with Schema, Query, Changeset, and Orm modules. However, it imports SqlBuilder, SqlFragment, SqlPart types from secure-composition via paths like '../../secure-composition/src/sql/builder'. This external dependency needs to be resolved for the project to build standalone.

Sources:
- 328d02d: import paths reference '../../secure-composition/src/sql/builder' and '../../secure-composition/src/sql/model'

nodes.title: How to include secure-composition dependency?
nodes.description: The ORM depends on secure-composition (a private repo providing SqlBuilder, SqlFragment, SqlPart, SqlString, SqlInt32, etc.). Since it's a private repo, it can't be fetched by a package manager. The question is how to make these types available to the ORM project.

nodes.title: Vendor entire secure-composition repo as nested project
nodes.description: Copy the entire secure-composition repository (HTML codec, CSS delegate, URL codec, path utilities, SQL builder, etc.) into the ORM project tree. Use Temper's file: dependency in package.json to reference it as a nested project. Build secure-composition first, then the ORM.

nodes.title: Include only the SQL subset of secure-composition
nodes.description: Instead of vendoring the entire secure-composition (which includes HTML, CSS, URL, path modules), extract only the SQL files (builder, model, imports, tests) that the ORM actually uses.

nodes.title: Vendor secure-composition and set up CI with Temper v0.6.0
nodes.description:
Vendored the entire secure-composition repository directly into the project tree (not as a git submodule). Set up GitHub Actions CI workflow that downloads Temper v0.6.0 Linux binary, builds secure-composition first, then builds and tests the ORM. Added package.json file: dependency for the nested layout. Fixed import paths from ../../secure-composition/ to ../secure-composition/.

This brought in ~10,000 lines including HTML codec, CSS delegate, URL codec, JS delegate, path utilities — only ~440 lines (SQL builder/model/imports/tests) are actually used by the ORM.

Sources:
- 3033637: 'Vendor secure-composition source directly (private repo, not a submodule)'
- 3033637: 'Fix import paths from ../../secure-composition/ to ../secure-composition/'
- 3033637: 'Add GitHub Actions workflow: downloads Temper v0.6.0 linux binary, builds secure-composition first, then builds and tests the ORM'

nodes.title: Temper v0.6.0 doesn't support @overload and generic interfaces
nodes.description:
CI failed because secure-composition uses language features (@overload annotations, generic interfaces) that aren't available in Temper v0.6.0. The code was developed against a dev build of Temper, not the stable release.

Sources:
- 96628b4: 'secure-composition uses features (@overload, generic interfaces) that aren't in v0.6.0. The code was developed against a dev build'

nodes.title: Which Temper version to use in CI?
nodes.description: Temper v0.6.0 (stable) lacks @overload and generic interfaces needed by secure-composition. Need to decide whether to rewrite secure-composition to avoid these features or use the nightly build.

nodes.title: Use Temper nightly (0.6.1-dev)
nodes.description: Switch to the nightly release which supports @overload and generic interfaces. Risk: nightly may have parser quirks or instability.

nodes.title: Rewrite secure-composition without @overload
nodes.description: Refactor secure-composition to avoid @overload and generic interfaces to stay on stable v0.6.0. High effort and may lose type safety.

nodes.title: Switch CI to Temper nightly (0.6.1-dev)
nodes.description:
Changed CI workflow to download Temper nightly (0.6.1-dev) instead of v0.6.0. This resolved the @overload and generic interface support.

Sources:
- 96628b4: 'Switch CI to Temper nightly (0.6.1-dev)' — changed download URL from v0.6.0 to nightly

nodes.title: Nightly bundles minimal JRE missing java.desktop module
nodes.description:
After switching to nightly, CI failed because the bundled JRE is minimal — missing java.desktop module which provides java.beans.Introspector needed by Temper.

Sources:
- 52b6488: 'The nightly temper bundles a minimal JRE missing java.desktop module'
- 2b6c38e: 'all required modules including java.beans.Introspector'

nodes.title: Add JDK 21 setup and investigate temper binary layout
nodes.description:
Added setup-java with temurin JDK 21 to CI. Added debug output to understand the temper binary structure (inspecting the bundled JRE layout). This was an investigatory step.

Sources:
- 52b6488: 'Add setup-java with temurin JDK 21 so JAVA_HOME is available. Also add debug output to understand the temper binary structure.'

nodes.title: Override temper launcher to use system JDK 21
nodes.description:
Created a wrapper script that overrides the temper launcher to use system JDK 21 (temurin) instead of the bundled minimal JRE. This provides all required Java modules including java.beans.Introspector.

Sources:
- 2b6c38e: 'Override the launcher script to use system JDK 21 (temurin) which has all required modules including java.beans.Introspector'

nodes.title: CI runs with nightly Temper using system JDK
nodes.description: CI now successfully runs Temper nightly (0.6.1-dev) by using system JDK 21 instead of the bundled minimal JRE. The @overload and generic interface features work. However, the build still uses secure-composition as a nested project, which creates project boundary issues.

nodes.title: Vendoring all of secure-composition confuses Temper's project boundary detection
nodes.description:
Temper's project boundary detection gets confused when secure-composition exists as a nested project. The two-project setup requires building secure-composition first, then the ORM — and the file: dependency in package.json creates complications. Only ~440 lines of the ~10,000 vendored lines are actually used (the SQL subset).

Sources:
- 551b566: 'Instead of vendoring all of secure-composition as a separate nested project (which confuses temper's project boundary detection)'

nodes.title: Inline only SQL files from secure-composition into src/sql/
nodes.description:
Removed the standalone secure-composition/ directory entirely. Moved only the SQL files (builder, model, imports, tests) into src/sql/. Updated import paths: src/*.temper.md now imports from ./sql/*, test/query_test.temper.md from ../src/sql/*. Removed the file: dependency from package.json.

This reduced the vendored code from ~10,000 lines to ~440 lines and made everything one Temper project.

Sources:
- 551b566: 'include only the SQL files (builder, model, imports, tests) directly under src/sql/. This makes everything one temper project with clean imports.'
- 551b566: 'Removed the standalone secure-composition/ directory and its file: dependency from package.json.'

nodes.title: Nightly parser has multiple incompatibilities with existing code
nodes.description:
After switching to Temper nightly (0.6.1-dev), several parser quirks were discovered:
1. Multi-line if/else blocks inside when arms fail to parse
2. ### markdown headers inside class bodies are treated as code block boundaries, breaking class method continuity
3. when expressions with semicolons yield Void type instead of chained expressions
4. Nullable Int32? not narrowed by null check on fields — requires local variable assignment

These issues didn't exist in v0.6.0 and required multiple code workarounds.

Sources:
- 68a4a5a: 'The temper nightly parser cannot handle multi-line if/else blocks inside when arms'
- 345137e: 'The Temper nightly (0.6.1-dev) parser treats ### markdown headers as code block boundaries, breaking class method continuity'
- e57869a: 'Convert when expressions to if-else chains (nightly parser compat)' and 'Fix nullable Int32? narrowing with local variable pattern'

nodes.title: Extract BoolField logic to parseBoolSqlPart() helper
nodes.description:
The nightly parser cannot handle multi-line if/else blocks inside when arms in the changeset's valueToSqlPart method. Extracted the bool-to-SqlBoolean conversion to a separate parseBoolSqlPart() method so the when arm is a single expression call instead of an inline block.

Sources:
- 68a4a5a: 'Extract the bool-to-SqlBoolean conversion to a separate parseBoolSqlPart() method so the when arm is a single expression call'

nodes.title: Replace ### headers with // comments inside class bodies
nodes.description:
The nightly parser treats ### markdown headers as code block boundaries. This broke class method continuity, causing parse errors like 'ClassName.if()' where the parser thought the class ended at the ### header. Replaced all ### headers inside class bodies with // inline comments across all source files (changeset, query, schema, sql/builder, sql/model).

Sources:
- 345137e: 'Replace all ### headers inside class bodies with // inline comments so each class is one continuous block'
- 345137e: 'causing parse errors like ClassName.if()'

nodes.title: Sibling modules process @I stage in parallel — cross-module imports fail
nodes.description:
Temper processes the @I (import) stage for sibling modules in parallel. This means src/ and src/sql/ are separate modules that can't import from each other during the import stage. The SQL files under src/sql/ can't be imported by src/*.temper.md because both modules are being processed simultaneously.

Sources:
- e57869a: 'Move sql/ files into src/ (sql_builder, sql_model, sql_imports, sql_tests) to avoid cross-module @I stage parallelism failures'

nodes.title: Flatten all files into single src/ module
nodes.description:
Comprehensive restructuring to make everything one flat module:

1. Added config.temper.md at project root to define 'orm' library (required by Temper)
2. Moved sql/ files into src/ with prefixed names: sql_builder, sql_model, sql_imports, sql_tests
3. Moved test files from test/ into src/ for same-module visibility (changeset_test, query_test, schema_test)
4. Removed all intra-module import statements — same-module files share scope automatically
5. Converted remaining when expressions to if-else chains (nightly parser compat)
6. Fixed nullable Int32? narrowing with local variable pattern: 'let lv = limitVal; if (lv != null) { ... }'
7. Renamed test helper sid→csid in changeset_test to avoid name collision (now sharing scope)

All 51 tests pass locally with 'temper test -b js'.

Sources:
- e57869a: Full commit message describes all changes
- e57869a: 'All 51 tests pass locally with temper test -b js'

nodes.title: All 51 tests pass — ORM fully working with flat module structure
nodes.description:
The ORM is now fully operational with a flat module structure:
- config.temper.md at root (library definition)
- All source files in src/: changeset, query, schema, orm, sql_builder, sql_model, sql_imports
- All test files in src/: changeset_test, query_test, schema_test, sql_tests
- No cross-module imports needed (same-module files share scope)
- Compatible with Temper nightly 0.6.1-dev parser
- CI uses system JDK 21 to run nightly Temper

The project went through three structural iterations:
1. Vendored secure-composition as nested project → project boundary confusion
2. Inlined SQL into src/sql/ subdirectory → cross-module import parallelism failure
3. Flattened everything into src/ → works

Sources:
- e57869a: 'All 51 tests pass locally with temper test -b js'

nodes.title: Explore multi-backend output (csharp, java, lua, py, rust)
nodes.description:
Added temper.keep/ name-selection files for additional backends: C#, Java, Lua, Python, Rust. This appears to be exploratory — testing Temper's multi-backend compilation capability beyond the JavaScript target used for testing.

Sources:
- ce6b2ae: 'lets see' — commit message suggests experimental exploration

edges.rationale: ORM works but has unresolved external dependency
Need to resolve external dependency on secure-composition
Initially chosen: vendor the whole repo
CI with v0.6.0 revealed feature gaps
Too much effort, loses type safety
JDK override resolved the runtime issue
Project boundary issues surfaced after CI was working
Project boundary issues led to inlining only SQL
Realized the SQL-subset approach was better
Switching to nightly introduced parser quirks
SQL-as-subdirectory approach revealed module parallelism issue
Additional parser fixes included in this commit
After ORM works, exploring other compilation targets
